title: Two booksellers, one warehouse
summary: After an Argentine novelist died, his unsold stock filled a warehouse. Two booksellers disagreed about what to do with it. Both were right about something.

body.speaker: Narrator
body.text: Hernán Diaz, an Argentine novelist, died in 2019. He left a warehouse in San Telmo with about twelve thousand unsold copies of his eleven books. His widow asked two booksellers, both his friends, to advise her. They could not agree.

body.speaker: Marta Rivas (independent bookshop owner)
body.text: Sell them slowly. Five hundred a year, maybe. At a small discount. They are good books. People who walk into a shop and see his name will remember him. If we dump them onto the market all at once, the price collapses and his estate gets almost nothing. Worse, his books become the kind of book that sits on a sale table for three pesos, which is how a writer is forgotten.

body.speaker: Tomás Linares (distributor)
body.text: I understand that, but I disagree. A warehouse costs money. Twelve thousand books take up space, and somebody must pay the rent. His widow cannot pay it forever. If we sell slowly, the warehouse eats the income before the income arrives. The honest thing is to clear the stock now, accept a low price, and put the money where it can help her.

body.speaker: Marta
body.text: But the price is not only money. A book on a discount table is read differently from a book on a normal shelf. People assume the cheap book is not very good. We would be teaching new readers that his work is second-rate. That insults him.

body.speaker: Tomás
body.text: And keeping twelve thousand copies in a warehouse, slowly going yellow, insults him too. Books are not relics. They are made to be read. A book that sits in a box for ten years has already failed at its job, whatever it costs.

body.speaker: Marta
body.text: That is true. But I have seen what happens when a small publisher floods the market after a death. Within two years, nobody mentions the writer. Within five, his name is gone from new catalogues. The dignified slow sale keeps him visible. Visibility is what allows the next reader to find him.

body.speaker: Tomás
body.text: Visibility for whom, though? You and I would still see those books on your shelf. But a young reader in Rosario, who has never heard of him, will not walk into your shop. She might find a discounted copy at a stall in her own city. The slow sale protects the writer's reputation among people who already knew him. The fast sale gives him new readers, even cheaply.

body.speaker: Marta
body.text: So we are protecting different things. You want him read. I want him taken seriously.

body.speaker: Tomás
body.text: Yes. And he is dead, so neither of us can ask him which he would prefer.

body.speaker: Narrator
body.text: His widow listened for two hours and made a compromise. Half of the stock would be sold quickly to clear the warehouse and pay her bills. The other half would be released slowly, through Marta's shop and a few others, over ten years. Five years later, the slow half is two-thirds gone. The fast half disappeared in eight months. New editions, requested by readers who first found him on a discount table, were printed last spring.

annotations.meaningVi: đổ ồ ạt ra thị trường
di sản (tài sản người đã mất)
thánh tích / vật cũ đáng giữ
việc bán chậm có phẩm giá
khả năng được nhìn thấy / sự hiện diện
catalogue mới / danh mục sách mới

grammarNotes.title: First and second conditional side by side
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 phân biệt câu điều kiện loại 1 (có thể xảy ra) và loại 2 (giả định / khó xảy ra). Loại 1 trong bài: 'If we dump them onto the market all at once, the price collapses.' — kết quả thực tế. Loại 2: 'A young reader in Rosario... might find a discounted copy' — 'might' diễn tả khả năng giả định. Cũng có cấu trúc 'would': 'We would be teaching new readers that his work is second-rate.' — kết quả không có thật.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 distinguishes first conditional (likely) from second (hypothetical). First in the passage: 'If we dump them onto the market all at once, the price collapses.' — real-world consequence. Hypothetical: 'A young reader in Rosario... might find a discounted copy' — 'might' marks a possible case. Also 'would': 'We would be teaching new readers that his work is second-rate.' — an unreal outcome.

translationVi:
Hernán Diaz là một tiểu thuyết gia Argentina thành công ở mức vừa phải. Ông mất năm 2019. Ông để lại một nhà kho ở San Telmo, Buenos Aires, chứa khoảng mười hai nghìn bản chưa bán được của mười một cuốn sách. Người vợ goá hỏi ý kiến hai người bán sách, cả hai từng là bạn ông. Họ không thể đồng ý với nhau.

Marta Rivas (chủ hiệu sách độc lập): Bán chầm chậm. Năm trăm cuốn một năm, có thể. Giảm giá ít. Sách hay. Người vào tiệm thấy tên ông sẽ nhớ ông. Nếu đổ ồ ạt ra thị trường, giá sụp đổ và di sản gần như không thu được gì. Tệ hơn, sách ông trở thành loại sách ngồi trên bàn hạ giá ba peso, tức là cách một nhà văn bị quên.

Tomás Linares (nhà phân phối): Tôi hiểu, nhưng tôi không đồng ý. Nhà kho tốn tiền. Mười hai nghìn cuốn chiếm chỗ, ai đó phải trả tiền thuê. Vợ ông không thể trả mãi. Nếu bán chậm, nhà kho ăn hết thu nhập trước khi thu nhập về. Việc trung thực là dọn kho ngay, chấp nhận giá thấp, và đưa tiền tới nơi giúp được bà.

Marta: Nhưng giá không chỉ là tiền. Một cuốn sách trên bàn hạ giá bị đọc khác với một cuốn trên kệ thường. Người ta cho rằng sách rẻ thì không hay. Ta đang dạy độc giả mới rằng tác phẩm của ông loại hai. Việc đó xúc phạm ông.

Tomás: Và giữ mười hai nghìn bản trong kho, từ từ ố vàng, cũng xúc phạm ông. Sách không phải di vật. Sách được làm ra để đọc. Một cuốn sách ngồi trong thùng mười năm đã thất bại nhiệm vụ, bất kể trị giá bao nhiêu.

Marta: Đúng vậy. Nhưng tôi đã thấy chuyện gì xảy ra khi một nhà xuất bản nhỏ tràn ngập thị trường sau khi tác giả mất. Trong hai năm, không ai nhắc đến nhà văn nữa. Trong năm năm, tên ông biến khỏi catalogue mới. Việc bán chậm có phẩm giá giữ ông được nhìn thấy. Sự hiện diện chính là điều cho phép độc giả tiếp theo tìm ra ông.

Tomás: Nhưng nhìn thấy cho ai? Anh và tôi sẽ vẫn thấy sách đó trên kệ của anh. Nhưng một độc giả trẻ ở Rosario, chưa từng nghe tên ông, sẽ không bước vào hiệu sách của anh. Cô ấy có thể tìm thấy bản hạ giá ở một quầy trong thành phố mình. Bán chậm bảo vệ danh tiếng với những người đã biết ông. Bán nhanh đem cho ông những độc giả mới, dù với giá rẻ.

Marta: Vậy hai ta đang bảo vệ những thứ khác nhau. Anh muốn ông được đọc. Tôi muốn ông được xem trọng.

Tomás: Đúng vậy. Và ông đã mất, nên không ai trong hai ta có thể hỏi ông muốn cái nào hơn.

Người vợ nghe hai tiếng và đi đến một thoả hiệp. Một nửa kho sẽ được bán nhanh để dọn chỗ và trả hoá đơn. Nửa kia sẽ được phát hành chậm, qua hiệu Marta và vài hiệu khác, trong mười năm. Năm năm sau, nửa chậm đã hết hai phần ba. Nửa nhanh biến trong tám tháng. Các tái bản mới, do các độc giả tìm thấy ông trên bàn hạ giá yêu cầu, đã được in mùa xuân năm ngoái.

questions.prompt: What did Hernán Diaz leave behind?
options: A warehouse with about 12,000 unsold copies of 11 books
Marta Rivas (independent bookshop owner) and Tomás Linares (distributor)
Sell slowly, around 500 a year; a flooded market makes a writer forgotten and second-rate
Clear the stock now; the warehouse rent eats the slow income; books are not relics

questions.prompt: Why does Marta say a book on a sale table is read differently?
options: Readers assume cheap = not very good, which insults the writer's reputation
Keeping unread books in a warehouse insults him too — 'books are not relics; they are made to be read'
He disagrees that writers can be insulted
He says cheap is fine if profitable

questions.prompt: What does Marta say happens after a flood-sale death?
options: Within 2 years nobody mentions him; within 5 his name is gone from new catalogues

questions.prompt: What does Tomás say a fast sale gives the writer?
options: New readers (e.g. a young reader in Rosario who would never enter Marta's shop), even cheaply

questions.prompt: What is Marta's final framing of the disagreement?
options: 'You want him read. I want him taken seriously.'

questions.prompt: What compromise did the widow choose?
options: Half sold quickly to clear the warehouse; half released slowly over 10 years
Slow half is two-thirds gone; fast half disappeared in 8 months; new editions reprinted last spring after discount-table readers asked
Two thoughtful people can disagree about a dead writer's legacy because they are protecting different goods — reputation vs. new readership — and a compromise can serve both.
questions.explanation: The whole shape of the dialogue.
questions.hint: Look at Marta's 'protecting different things' line.

cloze.template:
Narrator: Hernán Diaz, an Argentine novelist, died in 2019. He left a {{b1}} in San Telmo with about twelve thousand unsold copies of his eleven books. His {{b2}} asked two booksellers, both his friends, to advise her. They could not agree.
Marta Rivas (independent bookshop owner): Sell them slowly. Five hundred a year, maybe. At a small discount. They are good books. People who walk into a shop and see his name will remember him. If we dump them onto the market all at once, the price {{b3}} and his estate gets almost nothing. Worse, his books become the kind of book that sits on a sale table for three pesos, which is how a writer is forgotten.
Tomás Linares (distributor): I understand that, but I disagree. A warehouse costs money. Twelve thousand books take up space, and somebody must pay the rent. His widow cannot pay it forever. If we sell slowly, the warehouse eats the income before the income arrives. The honest thing is to clear the stock now, accept a low price, and put the money where it can help her.
Marta: But the price is not only money. A book on a discount table is read differently from a book on a normal shelf. People assume the cheap book is not very good. We would be teaching new readers that his work is {{b4}}. That insults him.
Tomás: And keeping twelve thousand copies in a warehouse, slowly going yellow, insults him too. Books are not {{b5}}. They are made to be read. A book that sits in a box for ten years has already failed at its job, whatever it costs.
Marta: That is true. But I have seen what happens when a small publisher floods the market after a death. Within two years, nobody mentions the writer. Within five, his name is gone from new {{b6}}. The dignified slow sale keeps him visible. Visibility is what allows the next reader to find him.
Tomás: Visibility for whom, though? You and I would still see those books on your shelf. But a young reader in {{b7}}, who has never heard of him, will not walk into your shop. She might find a discounted copy at a stall in her own city. The slow sale protects the writer's reputation among people who already knew him. The fast sale gives him new readers, even cheaply.
Marta: So we are protecting different things. You want him {{b8}}. I want him taken seriously.
Tomás: Yes. And he is dead, so neither of us can ask him which he would prefer.
Narrator: His widow listened for two hours and made a {{b9}}. Half of the stock would be sold quickly to clear the warehouse and pay her bills. The other half would be released slowly, through Marta's shop and a few others, over {{b10}} years. Five years later, the slow half is two-thirds gone. The fast half disappeared in eight months. New {{b11}}, requested by readers who first found him on a discount table, were printed last spring.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Marta and Tomás were 'protecting different things' — reputation vs. new readership. Think of someone whose work you admire (a writer, musician, craftsperson). If they died tomorrow with unsold stock, would you protect their reputation or their new readers, and why?
